docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The flow around a circular cylinder at Reynolds number Re = 100 is governed by the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations.

Since the objective of this project is to perform Reduced Order Modeling (ROM) using Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD), it is important to first understand the full-order mathematical model (FOM) from which the reduced model is derived.

In ROM, we do not directly solve the reduced equations first.

Instead, we follow:

Full CFD Simulation → Snapshot Generation → POD → Reduced Order Representation

This means the governing equations of the CFD simulation form the mathematical foundation of the entire ROM framework.

docs.title: Physical Assumptions
docs.text:
The simulation is based on the following assumptions:

  • Two-dimensional flow
  • Incompressible fluid
  • Laminar flow regime
  • Newtonian fluid
  • Constant density
  • Constant viscosity
  • Isothermal flow
  • No body forces
  • No heat transfer effects

Since the Reynolds number is:

            Re = 100

the flow lies in the laminar unsteady regime where wake instability and vortex shedding begin to appear.

This makes it an ideal benchmark problem for POD-based Reduced Order Modeling.

docs.title: Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation)
docs.text:
For incompressible flow, conservation of mass requires:

        ∇ · u = 0

where:

        u = (ux, uy)

is the velocity vector.

This equation ensures that the fluid volume remains conserved and no artificial compression occurs inside the computational domain.

Physically, this means:

Whatever fluid enters a control volume must also leave it.

This is the first condition that must always be satisfied in incompressible CFD.

docs.title: Momentum Equation (Navier–Stokes Equation)
docs.text:
The incompressible Navier–Stokes momentum equation is:

    ∂u/∂t + (u · ∇)u = -∇p + ν∇²u

where:

    - u = velocity vector
    - p = pressure
    - ν = kinematic viscosity
    - t = time

This equation represents Newton’s Second Law applied to fluid motion.

It balances:

    - acceleration
    - convection
    - pressure forces
    - viscous diffusion

and governs the entire flow physics around the cylinder.

docs.title: 1. Transient Term
docs.text:
∂u/∂t

This represents:

Change of velocity with time

This term is responsible for capturing:

    - unsteady flow behavior
    - wake development
    - vortex shedding
    - time-dependent flow structures

Without this term, the simulation would only capture steady-state flow.

Since cylinder wake flow is inherently unsteady, this term is essential.

docs.title: 2. Convective Term
docs.text:
(u · ∇)u

This represents:

Momentum carried by the moving fluid itself

This term is responsible for:

    - flow separation
    - wake formation
    - nonlinear flow behavior
    - vortex transport

This is the most important source of complexity in bluff-body external aerodynamics.

It is also the reason why vortex shedding becomes difficult to predict and why ROM becomes useful.

docs.title: 3. Pressure Gradient Term
docs.text:
-∇p

This represents:

Force generated by pressure differences

Pressure redistributes momentum throughout the domain and helps enforce incompressibility.

In Basilisk, pressure is solved using the pressure Poisson equation during pressure–velocity coupling.

This was also the main source of convergence warnings during the project.

docs.title: 4. Viscous Diffusion Term
docs.text:
ν∇²u

This represents:

Momentum diffusion caused by viscosity

It smooths the velocity field and resists strong velocity gradients.

At Re = 100, viscosity is still significant and strongly affects:

- wake stability
- boundary layer growth
- vortex formation

docs.title: Reynolds Number
docs.text:
The most important dimensionless parameter for this problem is the Reynolds number:

Re = UD/ν

where:

- U = free-stream inlet velocity
- D = cylinder diameter
- ν = kinematic viscosity

In this project:

Re = 100

This Reynolds number determines the competition between:

- inertial forces
- viscous forces

At this value:

- the wake becomes unstable
- recirculation appears
- periodic vortex shedding begins
- transient behavior becomes important

This is exactly why POD becomes meaningful.

If Re were too low, the flow would remain mostly steady and ROM would show only one dominant steady mode.

docs.title: Boundary Conditions
docs.text: Correct boundary conditions are critical for capturing realistic wake behavior.

docs.title: 1. Inlet Boundary
docs.text:
Uniform free-stream velocity is imposed:

ux = U0

uy = 0

This creates the incoming flow approaching the cylinder.

It represents an external flow problem rather than an internal confined flow like the lid-driven cavity.

docs.title: 2. Cylinder Surface
docs.text:
No-slip wall condition:

ux = 0

uy = 0

This creates:

  • boundary layer development
  • adverse pressure gradient
  • flow separation
  • wake formation

This is where the most important flow physics originate.

Without no-slip walls, there would be no vortex shedding.

docs.title: 3. Outlet Boundary
docs.text:
Outflow boundary condition:

∂u/∂n = 0

This allows the fluid to leave the computational domain naturally without artificial reflection.

It helps preserve realistic wake development behind the cylinder.

docs.title: 4. Top and Bottom Boundaries
docs.text:
Free-slip / open boundaries are used to avoid artificial confinement effects.

This allows the wake to develop naturally without strong wall interference.

docs.title: Embedded Boundary Method in Basilisk
docs.text:
Instead of generating a body-fitted mesh around the circular cylinder, Basilisk uses:

    "embed.h"

which applies the Embedded Boundary Method.

The cylinder geometry is defined using:

solid(cs, fs, sq(x) + sq(y) - sq(RADIUS));

where:

- cs = cell fraction
- fs = face fraction

This method allows:

- easy geometry representation
- Cartesian adaptive grids
- simpler meshing
- faster solver implementation
- robust boundary handling

This is one of the strongest advantages of Basilisk over traditional mesh-heavy CFD workflows.

docs.title: Connection to Reduced Order Modeling (ROM)
docs.text:
The Navier–Stokes equations define the Full Order Model (FOM) which is computationally expensive. A full CFD simulation may contain Thousands to millions of degrees of freedom and repeated solving becomes expensive. POD is applied to the CFD solution to build the:

Reduced Order Model (ROM), Instead of solving the full system repeatedly, ROM represents the flow using Few dominant spatial modes + temporal coefficients. This dramatically reduces computational cost while preserving the dominant system physics. This is the core purpose of this project.

docs.title: POD Mathematical Idea
docs.text:
The CFD snapshots are arranged into a matrix:

X = [x1  x2  x3  ...  xn]

where each column is one velocity snapshot.

Then Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is applied:

X = UΣVᵀ

where:

- U = spatial POD modes
- Σ = singular values (energy content)
- Vᵀ = temporal coefficients

This allows the flow to be written as:

Flow ≈ Mean + Few Dominant Modes

instead of solving the full Navier–Stokes equations every time.

This is how CFD becomes computationally efficient.

docs.title: Why POD is Useful Here
docs.text:
For cylinder flow, POD helps answer:

    What are the most energetic structures in the wake?  and
    Is the flow dominated by steady wake behavior or oscillatory vortex shedding?

In this project, POD showed:

- Mode 1 captured almost all system energy
- Higher modes were weak

This indicated that Mean wake structure dominated over oscillatory vortex shedding. This is a physically meaningful engineering conclusion.

docs.title: Final Insight
docs.text:
• The governing equations remain exactly the same.

  • ROM does not change the physics.

It changes only:

- How efficiently the physics is represented

- The Navier–Stokes equations provide the full physical truth.

- POD helps compress that truth into a smaller, faster, and more useful engineering model.

- That is the core philosophy of Reduced Order Modeling.

docs.title: Key Learnings
docs.text: This project provided significant insights into both CFD fundamentals and practical solver usage in OpenFOAM.

docs.title: 1. Solver Understanding (PIMPLE Algorithm)
docs.text:
• Learned how pressure-velocity coupling works in transient simulations
  • Understood the role of outer (PIMPLE) and inner (PISO/SIMPLE) loops
  • Realized that incorrect solver settings can lead to instability or divergence

👉 Key Insight: Solver configuration is not just syntax — it directly affects physical accuracy and stability

docs.title: 2. Turbulence Modeling (k-omega SST)
docs.text:
• Understood why k-omega SST is preferred for external aerodynamics
  • Learned that turbulence models require additional variables (k, omega)
  • Observed sensitivity of results to turbulence modeling

👉 Key Insight: Turbulence models are critical for predicting separation and wake behavior

docs.title: 3. Importance of Wall Distance (wallDist)
docs.text:
• Learned that wall distance is required for near-wall turbulence treatment
  • Understood how wall modeling affects boundary layer prediction

👉 Key Insight: Geometry-related quantities (like wall distance) are essential for turbulence closure

docs.title: 4. Numerical Stability (Courant Number)
docs.text:
• Observed simulation failure due to high Courant number
  • Learned to control time step using:
  • deltaT
  • adjustTimeStep
  • maxCo

👉 Key Insight: Numerical stability must be actively controlled in transient simulations

docs.title: 5. Discretization Schemes Matter
docs.text:
• Learned that missing schemes (e.g., div(phi,omega)) can crash the solver
  • Understood that different schemes affect numerical diffusion and accuracy

👉 Key Insight: Discretization is not just configuration — it influences solution quality

docs.title: 6. Function Objects and Post-Processing
docs.text:
• Learned how OpenFOAM computes forces using function objects
  • Understood the importance of correct placement (system/functions)

👉 Key Insight: Post-processing must be set up correctly during simulation, not after

docs.title: 7. Transient vs Steady Behavior
docs.text:
• Observed that flow remained transient even after 5 seconds
  • Learned concept of statistically steady state

👉 Key Insight: Unsteady flows cannot always be solved using steady-state assumptions

docs.title: 8. Mesh Quality and Accuracy
docs.text:
• Realized that coarse mesh leads to inaccurate drag prediction
  • Observed strong sensitivity of results to mesh resolution

👉 Key Insight: Mesh quality is one of the most critical factors in CFD accuracy

docs.title: 9. Drag Coefficient Sensitivity
docs.text:
• Learned that Cd depends on:
  • Reference area
  • Flow velocity
  • Mesh resolution
  • Wake prediction

👉 Key Insight: Even small mistakes in reference parameters can lead to large errors

docs.title: 10. Debugging Skills in OpenFOAM
docs.text:
• Learned to interpret error messages
  • Fixed issues related to:
  • solver dictionaries
  • turbulence setup
  • numerical schemes

👉 Key Insight: CFD requires strong debugging and problem-solving skills, not just theory

docs.title: Reduced Order Modeling of Flow Around a Circular Cylinder using POD in Basilisk
Problem Statement
docs.text:
The objective of this project is to simulate 2D incompressible flow around a circular cylinder at Reynolds number Re = 100 and develop a Reduced Order Model (ROM) using Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD).

Flow around a cylinder is one of the most classical benchmark problems in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) because it contains essential fluid physics such as:

  • boundary layer development
  • flow separation
  • wake formation
  • recirculation region
  • vortex shedding
  • transient unsteady flow behavior

Instead of only solving the full CFD problem, this project focuses on extracting the dominant coherent flow structures using POD and building the foundation for a computationally efficient reduced-order representation of the system.

The goal is not only simulation, but also physics compression.

docs.title: Engineering Relevance
docs.text:
Reduced Order Modeling is extremely important in engineering because high-fidelity CFD simulations are computationally expensive.

For real industrial applications such as:

  • aerodynamic design
  • thermal management
  • flow control
  • digital twins
  • optimization studies
  • real-time monitoring
  • design space exploration

running full CFD repeatedly becomes impractical.

ROM solves this problem by:

capturing the dominant physics of the system using only a small number of modes

This enables:

  • faster simulations
  • near real-time predictions
  • lower computational cost
  • rapid design iterations

Cylinder wake flow provides an ideal benchmark to study ROM because it contains both steady and transient flow structures.

docs.title: Motivation for This Project
docs.text:
The motivation for doing this project came from an important practical engineering problem:

High-fidelity CFD simulations are expensive and slow.

In industrial environments, engineers often need to perform:

  • multiple design iterations
  • optimization studies
  • parametric analysis
  • transient performance evaluation
  • real-time monitoring

Running full CFD for every case becomes computationally expensive and often impractical. This creates the need for Reduced Order Models (ROM). ROM allows engineers to preserve the dominant system physics while dramatically reducing computational cost.

docs.title: Why This Project After Lid-Driven Cavity?
docs.text:
The lid-driven cavity project was the first ROM project used to understand the fundamentals of:

  • snapshot generation
  • POD matrix construction
  • Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
  • mode extraction
  • temporal coefficient analysis

However, the lid-driven cavity problem is primarily:-

            Internal + confined + mostly steady flow

where the dominant structure is largely stable and symmetric. It is excellent for learning the mathematics of POD, but it does not fully represent the complexity of real engineering flows.

The cylinder flow project was chosen as the next step because it introduces:

        External + separated + transient + wake-dominated flow

which is much closer to industrial CFD problems. This project helps bridge the gap between: learning POD theory and Applying POD to realistic engineering physics

docs.title: Difference Between Lid-Driven Cavity and Cylinder ROM
docs.text:
Feature Lid-Driven Cavity Flow Around Cylinder Flow Type Internal flow External flow Geometry Closed square cavity Bluff body in open domain Driving Mechanism Moving top wall Inlet free stream Flow Nature Mostly steady / weak transient Strongly transient Dominant Physics Recirculation vortex Wake + vortex shedding POD Behavior One dominant steady mode Paired oscillatory modes expected Engineering Relevance Fundamental learning case Real industrial relevance ROM Complexity Beginner level Intermediate / advanced

The cavity project teaches:"How POD works"

The cylinder project teaches:"Why POD matters in real engineering"

docs.title: Low Reynolds Number
docs.text:
• attached flow
  • symmetric wake

docs.title: Moderate Reynolds Number (Re ≈ 100)
docs.text:
• wake instability
  • vortex shedding begins
  • periodic oscillations appear

docs.title: High Reynolds Number
docs.text:
• turbulent wake
  • strong nonlinear behavior

This makes the cylinder case an excellent candidate for understanding how POD captures coherent flow structures.

docs.title: What Was Done
docs.text:
In this project:

  • 2D incompressible Navier–Stokes equations were solved using Basilisk
  • the embedded boundary (embed.h) approach was used to represent the circular cylinder
  • the centered pressure–velocity coupled solver was used
  • transient flow simulation was performed up to t = 10
  • velocity snapshots were saved at regular intervals
  • Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) was applied using Python
  • Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) was used to extract dominant modes
  • temporal coefficients were analyzed
  • modal energy distribution was studied
  • dominant coherent structures were interpreted

The full workflow was:

```text CFD Simulation → Snapshot Generation → POD → Modal Analysis → Physical Interpretation

docs.title: Results — Reduced Order Modeling of Lid-Driven Cavity Flow
Snapshot Data
docs.text:
• Total snapshots generated: 101
  • Grid resolution: 64 × 64
  • Degrees of freedom: 8192 (u_x and u_y combined)

The dataset captures the transient evolution of the flow from rest to steady state.

docs.title: Energy Distribution of POD Modes
docs.text: The energy captured by each mode is computed using singular values obtained from SVD.

docs.title: Observed Distribution:
docs.text:
• Mode 1: 98.43%
  • Mode 2: 1.38%
  • Mode 3: 0.15%

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• The first mode dominates the system
  • The flow is highly low-dimensional
  • Most of the flow physics is captured by a single structure

This indicates that the lid-driven cavity flow at Re = 100 is governed by a dominant coherent vortex.

docs.title: Flow Reconstruction
docs.text: The flow field was reconstructed using a reduced number of modes.

docs.title: Reconstruction Setup:
docs.text: • Number of modes used: 5

docs.title: Error:
docs.text: • Relative reconstruction error ≈ 0.003859

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• High accuracy achieved with very few modes
  • Minimal loss of information despite strong dimensionality reduction
  • Confirms effectiveness of POD for this flow regime

docs.title: Spatial Modes
docs.text: The first POD mode represents the dominant flow structure.

docs.title: Observations:
docs.text:
• u_x mode shows strong shear near the moving lid
  • u_y mode captures vertical recirculation
  • The structure corresponds to the primary vortex inside the cavity

Higher modes represent minor corrections and localized effects.

docs.title: Temporal Coefficients
docs.text: Temporal coefficients describe how each mode evolves over time.

docs.title: Observations:
docs.text:
• Mode 1 increases and stabilizes
  • Modes 2 and 3 decay to zero

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• Flow starts from rest and develops over time
  • System reaches steady state
  • No sustained oscillations are present

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
The flow can be effectively represented as:

  • One dominant mode (primary vortex)
  • Small transient corrections

This confirms that the system is strongly low-dimensional.

docs.title: Limitations of Results
docs.text:
• Flow is steady and lacks dynamic richness
  • Temporal behavior is not oscillatory
  • Predictive ROM is limited in this case

docs.title: Overall Conclusion
docs.text:
• POD successfully reduces system dimensionality
  • A small number of modes capture most of the physics
  • Lid-driven cavity flow at low Reynolds number is highly compressible in modal space

This validates the use of POD for efficient representation of laminar flow systems.

docs.title: Flowchart — cavity.c Logic
docs.text:
1. Initialize simulation domain and grid
  2. Apply boundary conditions (moving lid + no-slip walls)
  3. Initialize velocity field to zero
  4. Start time-stepping loop:
  5. Update viscosity field
  6. Solve Navier–Stokes equations
  7. Export snapshots at fixed time intervals
  8. Advance time
  9. Repeat until final time is reached
  10. End simulation

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
The objective of this project is to simulate external flow over a 25° Ahmed body and estimate the drag coefficient.

The Ahmed body is a simplified car-like geometry widely used in automotive aerodynamics to study flow separation and wake behavior.

docs.title: Engineering Relevance
docs.text:
Understanding flow over bluff bodies is critical in automotive design because:

  • Drag directly affects fuel efficiency
  • Wake structure influences vehicle stability
  • Pressure distribution determines aerodynamic performance

The Ahmed body provides a benchmark case for validating CFD setups.

docs.title: What Was Done
docs.text:
In this project:

  • A transient CFD simulation was set up using OpenFOAM
  • The case was adapted from the pitzDaily tutorial
  • The PIMPLE algorithm was used for pressure-velocity coupling
  • The k-omega SST turbulence model was applied
  • The simulation was run up to 5 seconds to allow wake development
  • Forces were computed using OpenFOAM function objects

docs.title: Key Challenges
docs.text:
During the setup and execution, multiple issues were encountered:

  • PIMPLE dictionary undefined
  • Missing OmegaFinal entry
  • wallDist not defined
  • Missing div(phi,omega) term
  • meshWave typo in fvSchemes
  • Courant number instability
  • forces.dat not generated
  • Uncertainty in frontal area for Cd calculation

These challenges required debugging at both solver and physics levels.

docs.title: Flow Behavior Observed
docs.text:
• Flow separates at the slanted rear surface
  • A recirculation region forms behind the body
  • The wake remains unsteady
  • Drag force oscillates around a mean value

Even after 5 seconds, the flow remained transient but reached a statistically steady state.

docs.title: Results (Preliminary)
docs.text:
• Drag force ≈ 32 N
  • Estimated Cd ≈ 0.75

Expected Cd ≈ 0.3 (literature value)

docs.title: Initial Analysis
docs.text:
The higher drag coefficient is attributed to:

  • Coarse mesh (~40k tetrahedral cells)
  • Poor resolution of wake region
  • Lack of near-wall refinement

The solver setup is stable, but the solution is not yet grid-independent.

docs.title: Engineering Log
docs.text: The complete step-by-step development process is documented below:

docs.title: Drag Force
docs.text:
From the simulation (forces.dat):

  • Pressure drag ≈ 23–24 N
  • Viscous drag ≈ 7–8 N

Total drag force:

Fd ≈ 31 – 32 N

docs.title: Drag Coefficient Calculation
docs.text:
The drag coefficient is defined as:

Cd = Fd / (0.5 × rho × U² × A)

Where:

  • rho = 1.225 kg/m³
  • U = 25 m/s
  • A = 0.112 m² (Ahmed body frontal area)

docs.title: Step-by-Step Calculation
docs.text:
Dynamic pressure:

q = 0.5 × rho × U² = 0.5 × 1.225 × 25² ≈ 382.8 N/m²

Force normalization:

qA = 382.8 × 0.112 ≈ 42.9 N

Drag coefficient:

Cd = 32 / 42.9 ≈ 0.75

docs.title: Comparison with Literature
docs.text: Quantity Expected Value Obtained Value Drag Coefficient (Cd) ~0.30 ~0.75

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• The drag coefficient is significantly higher than expected
  • Flow field appeared stable after 5 seconds
  • Drag force oscillated around a mean value

docs.title: Wake Structure
docs.text:
The flow separates at the rear of the Ahmed body, forming a low-velocity wake region.

This recirculation zone contributes significantly to pressure drag.

docs.title: 1. Coarse Mesh
docs.text:
• Total cells ≈ 40k tetrahedral cells
  • Insufficient wake resolution
  • Poor capture of separation

docs.title: 2. Wake Not Properly Resolved
docs.text:
• Recirculation zone exaggerated
  • Base pressure too low
  • Leads to higher pressure drag

docs.title: 3. Lack of Near-Wall Refinement
docs.text:
• Boundary layer not captured properly
  • Separation point inaccurate

docs.title: 4. No Grid Independence Study
docs.text:
• Only one mesh used
  • Results not validated

docs.title: Flow Nature
docs.text:
• Flow remains transient
  • Drag fluctuates over time
  • Mean value stabilizes

→ This is a statistically steady flow, not a steady-state solution

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
• Solver setup is correct
  • Simulation is stable
  • Error mainly comes from mesh quality

→ Physics is captured qualitatively, not quantitatively accurate yet
